subtitle: When Silent Pillars Found Their Tongue Again

panels.shot: dramatic low-angle wide of James Prinsep before a towering Ashokan pillar in mist
panels.action: James Prinsep stands in early-morning Calcutta light, hand resting on a weathered Ashokan pillar segment, his face intent, as ghostly silhouettes of distant rocks and pillars fade into the misted horizon behind him.

dialogue.text: In the age of steam and empire, an English scholar bends over ancient Indian stone, and the lost voice of Ashoka begins to stir.

i18n.hi: भाप और साम्राज्य के दौर में, एक अंग्रेज़ विद्वान प्राचीन भारतीय पत्थर पर झुकता है, और अशोक की खोई आवाज़ जाग उठती है।

panels.shot: bird's-eye view of Calcutta’s Asiatic Society building and bustling streets
panels.action: From above, the white colonnades of the Asiatic Society of Bengal rise amid crowded Calcutta streets, carts and palanquins moving, as James Prinsep hurries up the steps clutching rolled tracings.

dialogue.text: Calcutta, 1830s. The Asiatic Society is a crossroads of manuscripts, coins and stones, where the ruins of many kingdoms arrive, but few are understood.

i18n.hi: कलकत्ता, 1830 का दशक। एशियाटिक सोसाइटी पांडुलिपियों, सिक्कों और पत्थरों का चौराहा है, जहाँ कई राजों के खंडहर आते हैं, पर बहुत कम समझे जाते हैं।

panels.shot: medium interior shot of Prinsep at a long table crowded with rubbings and sketches
panels.action: Inside a dim, high-ceilinged hall, James Prinsep leans over a long table strewn with paper rubbings of inscriptions, ink, and copper coins, a single lamp throwing sharp light on the strange Brahmi letters.

dialogue.text: James Prinsep, secretary of the Society, engraver by trade, gives his life to weights, measures, coins and scripts, chasing order in a sea of fragments.

i18n.hi: सोसाइटी के सचिव जेम्स प्रिन्सेप, पेशे से नक़्क़ाश, अपना जीवन बाटों, नापों, सिक्कों और लिपियों को देते हैं—टुकड़ों के समुद्र में व्यवस्था खोजते हुए।

panels.shot: tight overhead shot of Prinsep’s hands arranging multiple inscription facsimiles side by side
panels.action: Prinsep’s ink-stained fingers carefully align multiple small inscription facsimiles from Sanchi and Bharhut, the repeated clusters of Brahmi letters circled and underlined in his precise hand.

dialogue.text: Across India stand pillars and rails cut with tiny letters in a lost script. For centuries they have been scenery. To Prinsep, they are a code waiting for patience.

i18n.hi: भारत भर में स्तंभ और रेलिंगें खड़ी हैं, खोई हुई लिपि के छोटे अक्षरों से कटी हुई। सदियों से वे बस नज़ारा हैं। प्रिन्सेप के लिए वे धैर्य से खुलने वाला कोड हैं।

panels.shot: close-up on Prinsep’s face lit by lamplight, eyes narrowed in concentration
panels.action: By lamplight, sweat on his brow, James Prinsep peers through a magnifier, lips moving silently as he traces the same little knot of letters appearing again and again at the end of short rail inscriptions.

dialogue.text: From Sanchi and Bharhut come dozens of brief donor texts. Prinsep notices one cluster always at the end. A small gift from ancient hands: repetition.

i18n.hi: सांची और भरहुत से दर्जनों छोटे दान-लेख आते हैं। प्रिन्सेप देखता है कि एक झुंड हमेशा अंत में आता है। प्राचीन हाथों की छोटी देन: दोहराव।

panels.shot: insert shot of a Sanchi-style rail fragment with Brahmi letters highlighted in his notes
panels.action: On his desk lies a careful drawing of a Sanchi rail post, the Brahmi line running along its curve, with the final word copied beneath; beside it, Prinsep has pencilled a tentative Latin transliteration with question marks.

dialogue.text: By comparing forms, he begins to suspect sounds. This ending might be dāna, gift, a word still living in the tongues around him.

i18n.hi: रूपों की तुलना कर वह ध्वनियों का अंदाज़ा लगाना शुरू करता है। यह अंत शायद ‘दान’ होगा—एक शब्द जो उसके आस-पास की ज़ुबानों में अब भी जिंदा है।

panels.shot: medium shot of Prinsep addressing a small circle of Indian scribes and assistants
panels.action: In the Society office, Prinsep stands beside a chalkboard of Brahmi letters while Indian munshis and pandits sit on mats, one pointing from the script to a modern Devanagari word in a palm-leaf manuscript.

dialogue.text: Prinsep does not work alone. Indian scribes, Brahmin scholars, draftsmen and surveyors shape his access, his copies, his ideas of sound and word.

i18n.hi: प्रिन्सेप अकेले नहीं है। भारतीय मुंशी, ब्राह्मण विद्वान, नक़्शानवीस और सर्वेयर उसकी पहुँच, उसकी नकलों और उसकी ध्वनि–शब्द की समझ को गढ़ते हैं।

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder shot of Prinsep reading a letter with coin sketches from Europe
panels.action: Prinsep sits near an open window, holding a letter bearing neat sketches of Indo-Greek and early Indian coins, the name ‘Christian Lassen’ visible in the signature, as he glances from coin legend to Brahmi list.

dialogue.text: In Bonn, Christian Lassen studies bilingual coins. From their Greek and Indian legends he suggests sound-values. Across continents, alphabets talk to each other.

i18n.hi: बॉन में क्रिश्चियन लैसन द्विभाषी सिक्कों को पढ़ता है। यूनानी और भारतीय लिखावट से वह ध्वनि-मूल्य सुझाता है। महाद्वीपों के पार वर्णमालाएँ एक–दूसरे से बातें करती हैं।

panels.shot: medium shot of Prinsep and a younger survey officer poring over a pillar rubbing
panels.action: At a side table, Prinsep and a young Alexander Cunningham bend over a large rubbing of an Ashokan pillar inscription, Cunningham’s finger running along the grooves while Prinsep annotates in careful script.

dialogue.text: Engineers and surveyors like Alexander Cunningham send rubbings of distant pillars. Bit by bit, the same alphabet appears from Delhi to Allahabad, from Sanchi to Sahasram.

i18n.hi: एलेक्ज़ांडर कनिंघम जैसे इंजीनियर और सर्वेयर दूर के स्तंभों की रबिंग भेजते हैं। धीरे–धीरे वही अक्षरमाला दिल्ली से इलाहाबाद, सांची से सहसराम तक दिखती है।

panels.shot: slow push-in on a long Ashokan edict rubbing pinned to the wall
panels.action: Pinned across the wall stretches a long rubbing of an Ashokan rock edict, dark letters marching in lines; above several repeated phrases, Prinsep has stuck small paper slips with guessed sounds.

dialogue.text: By 1837, the puzzle begins to yield. Letters are no longer mute shapes. Words like rājā, dhamma, prajā emerge, faint but recognisable.

i18n.hi: १८३७ तक पहेली ढीली पड़ने लगती है। अक्षर अब गूँगे आकार नहीं रहे। ‘राजा’, ‘धम्म’, ‘प्रजा’ जैसे शब्द उभरते हैं—हल्के, पर पहचान में आने वाले।

panels.shot: close-up on the phrase ‘Devanampiya Piyadasi’ as Brahmi characters and his transliteration below
panels.action: On a sheet spread before him, the Brahmi characters for De-va-nam-pi-ya Pi-ya-da-si stand carefully inked, and beneath them Prinsep has written ‘Devanampiya Piyadasi’, underlined twice.

dialogue.text: Most striking is a royal formula: Devanampiya Piyadasi—Beloved of the Gods, of gracious appearance. This king speaks from many stones, yet no known history names him.

i18n.hi: सबसे चौंकाने वाली शाही पंक्ति है: देवनाम्पिय पियदसि—देवताओं के प्रिय, सुंदर रूप वाले। यह राजा कई पत्थरों से बोलता है, पर कोई इतिहास उसका नाम नहीं जानता।

panels.shot: medium shot of Prinsep presenting his paper to a small learned gathering at the Society
panels.action: In the Society hall, Prinsep stands at a lectern holding a manuscript, while a row of European and Indian scholars listen, some leaning forward, as sheets of the Brahmi alphabet are displayed behind him.

dialogue.speaker: james_prinsep
dialogue.text: Gentlemen, the ancient character of these edicts is now, I trust, in great part restored.

i18n.hi: सज्जनो, मुझे भरोसा है कि इन अभिलेखों का प्राचीन रूप अब बड़े हिस्से में लौट आया है।

panels.shot: insert close-up of the printed Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal with his article
panels.action: A freshly printed volume of the Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal lies open, the title of Prinsep’s article visible above columns of Brahmi letters matched to sounds, a faint press-smell in the air.

dialogue.text: In 1837 and 1838 he publishes his Brahmi alphabet. For the first time in over a thousand years, the script of Ashoka’s India can be read again.

i18n.hi: १८३७–३८ में वह अपनी ब्राह्मी वर्णमाला छापता है। हज़ार साल से ज़्यादा बाद पहली बार अशोक के भारत की लिपि फिर पढ़ी जा सकती है।

panels.shot: wide shot of a Sri Lankan monk in a monastic library reading the Mahavamsa
panels.action: In a lamp-lit Sri Lankan monastery, a monk in saffron robes bends over a Pali palm-leaf manuscript of the Mahavamsa, another European scholar beside him taking notes in a bound book.

dialogue.text: Yet the king behind Piyadasi remains unnamed, until George Turnour studies the Pali Mahavamsa in Ceylon and finds a mirror there.

i18n.hi: पर पियदसि के पीछे का राजा अब भी बिना नाम है, जब तक जॉर्ज टर्नर सीलोन में पाली महावंस पढ़कर उसमें एक आईना नहीं पाता।

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder shot of Turnour’s notes comparing Mahavamsa passages to edict titles
panels.action: On a table, Turnour’s notebook shows Pali lines naming a ruler ‘Devanampiya Piyadasi’ alongside his English note: ‘identified with King Asoka’, arrows linking text to the printed edicts from Calcutta.

dialogue.text: The chronicle praises a king Asoka, styled Devanampiya Piyadasi, who spreads dhamma. Turnour joins text to stone and writes to Prinsep with the link.

i18n.hi: वृत्तांत एक राजा ‘असोक’ की स्तुति करता है, जिसका उपाधि है देवनाम्पिय पियदसि, जो धम्म फैलाता है। टर्नर पाठ को पत्थर से जोड़ता है और यह सूत्र प्रिन्सेप को लिख भेजता है।

panels.shot: close-up on Prinsep reading Turnour’s letter, a flicker of realization in his eyes
panels.action: Back in Calcutta, Prinsep sits at his cluttered desk unfolding Turnour’s letter, his eyes widening as he scans the passage that equates Devanampiya Piyadasi with King Ashoka.

dialogue.text: The revelation is simple yet immense. The unseen author of the edicts is not a shadow-king, but Ashoka of the Mauryas, long half-remembered in legend.

i18n.hi: यह खुलासा सीधा है, पर विराट। अभिलेखों का अदृश्य लेखक कोई छाया–राजा नहीं, बल्कि मौर्य अशोक है, जो लोककथाओं में आधा–आधा याद था।

panels.shot: medium shot of Prinsep pinning a note labelled ‘Ashoka’ beside an edict rubbing
panels.action: Prinsep rises and pins a small slip bearing the name ‘Ashoka’ beside the heading ‘Devanampiya Piyadasi’ on his wall-length rubbing, then steps back, shoulders sagging with a mix of fatigue and quiet triumph.

dialogue.speaker: james_prinsep
dialogue.text: So, Ashoka… it is your own hand that has written across these centuries.

i18n.hi: तो अशोक… यह तुम्हारा अपना हाथ है जिसने सदियों पर यह लेख लिख दिया।

panels.shot: wide montage shot of various Ashokan sites across India under different skies
panels.action: A sweeping montage shows the Sanchi stupa railings, the Delhi-Topra pillar, a rock face at Girnar, and a distant boulder at Maski under an overcast sky, all bearing faintly carved lines of Brahmi letters.

dialogue.text: More than thirty edicts are now recognised: on pillars in cities, on lonely rocks in the countryside, reaching even to Kandahar where Greek and Aramaic share Ashoka’s frontier voice.

i18n.hi: अब तीस से ज़्यादा अभिलेख पहचाने गए हैं—शहरों के स्तंभों पर, जंगलों की चट्टानों पर, दूर कंधार तक जहाँ यूनानी और अरामी में भी अशोक की सीमा–आवाज़ गूँजती है।

panels.shot: ground-level shot at Maski showing the later-discovered inscription in rugged landscape
panels.action: In a rugged Deccan landscape decades later, surveyors stand before the Maski rock, its weathered inscription panel catching the sun, while one officer traces the specific word ‘Asoka’ cut into the stone.

dialogue.text: In 1915, at Maski, a rock edict names Asoka directly. The later find confirms the 1830s insight: Devanampiya Piyadasi and Ashoka are one.

i18n.hi: १९१५ में मसकी पर एक शिला–अभिलेख ‘असोक’ नाम से सीधे बोलता है। यह बाद की खोज १८३० के दशक की समझ को पक्का करती है: देवनाम्पिय पियदसि और अशोक एक ही हैं।

panels.shot: intimate close-up of Prinsep, older and weary, sitting by a window with distant gaze
panels.action: An older, visibly fatigued Prinsep sits by an open window in Calcutta, papers stacked beside him, gazing out at the hazy city as monsoon clouds gather, his hands resting on a closed notebook of Brahmi letters.

dialogue.text: Prinsep does not live long after his decipherment. Overwork and illness break him in 1840, but the key he forged is already in many hands.

i18n.hi: अपनी पढ़त के कुछ ही समय बाद प्रिन्सेप ज़्यादा नहीं जीता। हद से ज़्यादा काम और बीमारी १८४० में उसे तोड़ देते हैं, पर उसकी गढ़ी हुई कुंजी अब कई हाथों में है।

panels.shot: wide present-day shot of Indian students and visitors walking around Sanchi stupa
panels.action: Under a clear modern sky, students and visitors circle the stupa at Sanchi, some pausing by the railing inscriptions as a guide gestures toward the Brahmi lines etched into the ancient stone.

dialogue.text: Today, travellers read translations of those lines. Through Prinsep and his network, Ashoka’s own words on dhamma and rule return to public life.

i18n.hi: आज यात्री उन पंक्तियों के अनुवाद पढ़ते हैं। प्रिन्सेप और उसके जाल के ज़रिये अशोक के धम्म और राज के अपने शब्द फिर लोक–जीवन में लौट आए हैं।

panels.shot: final contemplative shot of an Ashokan lion capital against a twilight sky
panels.action: Against a deepening twilight, an Ashokan lion capital crowns a pillar, its polished stone lions facing the four directions as the land below slowly darkens into silhouette.

dialogue.text: Stone once silent now speaks of a king, an empire and an ethic. Patient eyes, many hands and careful minds have made the forgotten king audible again.

i18n.hi: जो पत्थर कभी गूँगे थे, अब एक राजा, एक साम्राज्य और एक नीति की बात करते हैं। धैर्यभरी नज़रों, कई हाथों और सजग दिमागों ने भूले राजा को फिर सुनने लायक बना दिया है।

subtitle_i18n.en: When Silent Pillars Found Their Tongue Again
subtitle_i18n.hi: जब खामोश स्तंभों ने फिर ज़ुबान पाई
